title: Binance signed-URL with HMAC signature now logged on every fapi network failure (regression vs. pre-sweep silent path)
summary: Pre-sweep, the bare `except: pass` around the Binance `fapiPrivate_get_income` block silently swallowed network-class ccxt failures (`RequestTimeout`, `ExchangeNotAvailable`, `ExchangeError` from `aiohttp.ClientError`). Post-sweep, the new `logger.warning('Binance futures-income failed (falling back to BTC spot trades): %s', exc, exc_info=True)` writes the exception's `str(exc)` into Railway stdout AND into Sentry breadcrumbs. For ccxt's network exception class, the exception message is constructed at `ccxt/async_support/base/exchange.py:271-285` as `details = ' '.join([self.id, method, url])` — and for Binance signed GETs (which fapiPrivate_get_income is), the URL is built at `ccxt/async_support/binance.py:11538-11543` as `url += '?' + query` where `query` ends with `&signature=<HEX-HMAC-SHA256>`. The new WARNING therefore emits the HMAC-SHA256 signature on every Binance fapi timeout/DNS/connection-reset. The pre-existing `validate_key_permissions` exception handlers log only ccxt.AuthenticationError-class exceptions (which carry the response body, NOT the URL) — this sweep is the FIRST site to expose network-class URLs on a recurring (cron-scheduled) sync path. Defense-in-depth violation: HMAC signature is not the secret itself, but a known-plaintext + MAC pair lands in logs.
evidence: ccxt source `analytics-service/.venv/lib/python3.14/site-packages/ccxt/async_support/base/exchange.py` lines 271-285: `details = ' '.join([self.id, method, url])` for all 4 network-class exceptions. ccxt source `analytics-service/.venv/lib/python3.14/site-packages/ccxt/async_support/binance.py` line 11538: `query += '&' + 'signature=' + signature`. Line 11543: `url += '?' + query`. Sentry breadcrumb format at `sentry-sdk/integrations/logging.py:369-377` stores `record.message` (the formatted log string with signature) into `breadcrumb['message']` field — and `analytics-service/sentry_init.py:_redact_before_send` only scrubs `breadcrumbs[*]['data']`, NOT `breadcrumbs[*]['message']`. Confirmed by reading sentry_init.py L224-L232.
fix: Two options, low-overhead: (1) redact `exc` via `services.redact.scrub_freeform_string(str(exc))` before logging, OR (2) log only `type(exc).__name__` plus a sanitized message. Recommended concrete change: replace `logger.warning('Binance futures-income failed (falling back to BTC spot trades): %s', exc, exc_info=True)` with `logger.warning('Binance futures-income failed (falling back to BTC spot trades), exc_class=%s', type(exc).__name__, exc_info=True)`. Python's `traceback.format_exception` (the implementation behind `exc_info=True`) DOES emit the exception's `__str__` as the first line — so even with the format-arg change, the signature would still appear in the traceback's first line. Stronger fix: catch network-class ccxt errors specifically (`ccxt.NetworkError`) and log only the exchange.id, suppressing the traceback's str(exc) line via a custom formatter. Minimal acceptable: at minimum apply `services.redact.scrub_freeform_string` to `str(exc)` before logging AND drop `exc_info=True` for the network-class branch.

title: Bybit closed_pnl WARNING also passes `exc_info=True` — but Bybit V5 puts signature in headers, not URL
summary: Same `logger.warning('Bybit closed_pnl fetch / ISO-conversion failed: %s', exc, exc_info=True)` pattern as the Binance site. For ccxt Bybit V5 (this branch calls `private_get_v5_position_closed_pnl`), the signing scheme places HMAC in the `X-BAPI-SIGN` HTTP HEADER, not in the URL — see `ccxt/async_support/bybit.py` sign() method's `isV5UnifiedAccount` branch. The URL `?category=linear&limit=200` contains no secrets. Therefore: NOT a credential leak for the V5 path. However, the WARNING DOES still expose: (a) the exception class and (b) any response body string that ccxt placed in the exception (Bybit error bodies sometimes include retCode/retMsg with no PII). Lower confidence because the V5 surface is benign as-coded; the finding is preventative for a future refactor that switches to a legacy Bybit path (which DOES embed api_key+timestamp+sign in URL — see `ccxt/async_support/bybit.py` else-branch of sign()).
evidence: `ccxt/async_support/bybit.py` sign() function: V5 isUnifiedAccount branch builds `url += '?' + queryEncoded` from `params` only; signature lives in `headers['X-BAPI-SIGN']`. The else-branch (non-V5/non-OpenAPI legacy) DOES embed `'api_key': self.apiKey, 'recv_window': ..., 'timestamp': ..., 'sign': signature` into the URL via `keysort(query)` + rawencode — that path is not used here today. The current `private_get_v5_position_closed_pnl` call is safe.
fix: Defensive: same redaction pattern as the Binance fix above. Not blocking — apply if/when applying the Binance fix as one PR. Optionally pin a test that asserts the call site is `private_get_v5_position_closed_pnl` (so a future migration to a legacy Bybit endpoint surfaces here).

title: Sentry `before_send` scrubber walks `breadcrumbs[*]['data']` only — `['message']` field is un-scrubbed (pre-existing gap, sweep's new WARNINGs land in it)
summary: Pre-existing defense-in-depth gap, not introduced by this sweep, but the sweep's new fail-loud WARNINGs now ride through it. The Sentry before_send hook scrubs `breadcrumbs[*]['data']` (line 232: `crumb['data'] = _scrub(crumb['data'])`) but does NOT touch `breadcrumbs[*]['message']` — which is where Sentry's LoggingIntegration puts `record.getMessage()` (the format-string-interpolated WARNING text). The Binance signed-URL finding above lands here: when a downstream ERROR-level event triggers a Sentry capture, the prior WARNING breadcrumb (with the signed URL in its `message`) attaches to the event and is sent to Sentry un-scrubbed. The sweep is the first reason to fix this gap, but the fix belongs in `sentry_init.py`, not in the sweep.
evidence: `analytics-service/sentry_init.py` lines 227-232: walks `event['breadcrumbs']['values'][*]['data']` only. `sentry-sdk/integrations/logging.py:370-376` shows the breadcrumb dict shape: `{'type': 'log', 'level': ..., 'category': ..., 'message': record.message, 'timestamp': ..., 'data': self._extra_from_record(record)}` — message is a top-level breadcrumb field, not under data.
fix: In `_redact_before_send`, extend the breadcrumbs walker to also scrub `crumb.get('message')` via `services.redact.scrub_freeform_string(crumb['message'])` (which applies the `key: value` substring redactor — line 80-90 of redact.py: matches `api[-_]?key`, `api[-_]?secret`, `signature`, `token`, etc.). This is a separate PR — sweep should not change Sentry scrubbing surface.

title: `logger.warning(..., exc_info=True)` traceback frame summaries do NOT include local variable values by default — partial mitigation
summary: Defense-in-depth note. Python's `logging.Formatter.formatException` → `traceback.format_exception` emits the exception chain + stack frame summaries (filename, lineno, code line) WITHOUT local variable values. So `exc_info=True` does not directly leak ccxt's `apiKey`/`secret` even though the `exchange` object lives in the failing frame's locals. The leak surface is purely `str(exc)` (the first line of the exc_info output). Sentry's `with_locals=True` (the SDK default; `send_default_pii=False` at `sentry_init.py:293` is orthogonal) would attach frame.vars on event-level captures — but WARNINGs are breadcrumbs only (LoggingIntegration default `event_level=ERROR`), so frame.vars are NOT serialized for the sweep's new WARNINGs. The outer fetch_daily_pnl wrapper's `logger.error('fetch_daily_pnl failed: %s', str(e))` at L620-L621 DOES use ERROR-level — but it logs `str(e)` only (no exc_info), so Sentry would capture an event with auto-collected frame.vars and the `_redact_before_send` PII scrubber at sentry_init.py:237-251 walks `exception[*].stacktrace.frames[*].vars` and `_scrub`s them — coverage is good for the `exchange` object (its `__repr__` returns just `ccxt.async_support.binance()`, no apiKey leak — see `ccxt/base/exchange.py:486-487`).
evidence: Python 3 stdlib `traceback.format_exception()`: does not emit locals by default. `sentry_init.py:237-251` walks frame.vars and applies `_scrub`. `ccxt/base/exchange.py:486-487` shows Exchange `__repr__` returns only `'ccxt.' + ('async_support.' if ...) + self.id + '()'`.
fix: None required for sweep — existing PII-scrub coverage for the outer ERROR is correct. Document only.

title: Cron / authenticated entry points throttle log volume — DoS-to-log-sink risk bounded
summary: Mandate question: a previously-best-effort path now logs — can an attacker trigger the WARNING repeatedly to overwhelm Railway/Sentry sinks? Reviewed entry points: `routers/exchange.py:131` (POST `/exchange/sync` requires JWT auth + strategy ownership check at L115-L116), `routers/cron.py:150` (cron-scheduled, INTERNAL_API_TOKEN gated at the cron handler), `routers/portfolio.py:992` (portfolio sync route, auth required), `services/job_worker.py:529` (background worker, no external trigger). Authenticated triggers + cron rate-limit the volume. Sentry has 0.1 traces_sample_rate at `sentry_init.py:292` which damps event volume; breadcrumb volume is unaffected by traces_sample_rate (breadcrumbs only attach when an event fires). Railway's stdout-log buffer is generous. No realistic DoS path to log sink from this sweep alone.
evidence: routers/exchange.py:115-116: `if api_key_row.data.get('user_id') != strategy_result.data.get('user_id'): raise HTTPException(status_code=403, ...)`. routers/cron.py:13 imports `validate_internal_token` (INTERNAL_API_TOKEN gate). sentry_init.py:292: traces_sample_rate=0.1.
fix: None.

title: `fetch_daily_pnl` outer ERROR cascade fires only when both primary and fallback paths fail — no auth-bypass info leak
summary: Mandate question: any of the new raises happen BEFORE an auth check, allowing an unauthenticated client to glean information from the error response? Reviewed: `fetch_daily_pnl` is called only AFTER auth in `routers/exchange.py` (JWT + strategy ownership at L115-L116), `routers/cron.py` (INTERNAL_API_TOKEN), `routers/portfolio.py` (auth), and `services/job_worker.py` (no external trigger). The outer wrapper at L620 returns `daily_pnl = []` on cascade — never propagates `str(e)` to an HTTP response. Routes that call `fetch_all_trades` (which calls fetch_daily_pnl) wrap with their own `try/except` → `raise HTTPException(500, detail='Failed to fetch trades from exchange')` (routers/exchange.py:133-134). The detail is a generic English string, not the underlying exc message. No info leak to client.
evidence: routers/exchange.py:130-134: `try: trades = await fetch_all_trades(...) ... except Exception: raise HTTPException(500, detail='Failed to fetch trades from exchange')`. exchange.py:620-621: `except Exception as e: logger.error('fetch_daily_pnl failed: %s', str(e))` — log only, no return propagation.
fix: None.

title: metrics.py WARNINGs interpolate `exc` + `returns_len` only — no user-controlled strings, no PII surface
summary: Reviewed all 11 new `logger.warning(...)` calls in compute_all_metrics. Format args are: scalar name (literal string), `returns_len_for_log` (int), `len(monthly_rets)` (int), and `exc` (a qstats/pandas/numpy exception). The `returns` Series carries float values from analytics_runner (post-decryption, post-position-reconstruction — no API keys / user emails / encryption_key / KEK). qstats/pandas/numpy exception messages are operationally bounded (e.g. 'cannot convert NaN to integer', 'array empty', 'operands could not be broadcast'). No user-controlled string interpolation, no f-string injection, no PII surface. Confirmed by reading metrics.py:225-572 and analytics_runner.py:1142-1143.
evidence: All 11 sites at metrics.py:350-353, 358-361, 384-388, 392-395, 399-403, 407-411, 415-419, 423-427, 431-434, 439-442, 470-472, 564-568 use `returns_len_for_log, exc, exc_info=True` (or +`len(monthly_rets)` for var_1m_99 at L387). `compute_all_metrics(returns, benchmark_returns)` signature at L225 — both args are pandas Series of floats.
fix: None.

title: `logger.exception` pattern in `validate_key_permissions` already established — sweep extends but does not regress the surface
summary: Mandate question: does any new fail-loud site log data that previously didn't appear in logs? Reviewed: the existing `validate_key_permissions` handlers at L274-L342 use `logger.exception('... on %s — %s', exchange.id, exc)` for AuthenticationError, DDoSProtection, RateLimitExceeded, ExchangeNotAvailable, NetworkError. These ccxt classes' messages are constructed from response BODY (`feedback = self.id + ' ' + body` in binance.py:11616/11626/11628) — NOT the signed URL. So the pre-existing `logger.exception` calls don't expose the URL. The NEW sweep's `except Exception` (catch-all) at exchange.py:552 (Binance) catches BOTH response-body-class errors AND network-class errors (`RequestTimeout` etc), the latter of which DO embed the URL. This is the regression captured by Finding #1. Pre-existing risk: bounded to response-body content (operationally benign). New risk: extends to URL+signature.
evidence: exchange.py:274-342 shows existing pattern. ccxt/async_support/binance.py:11616-11628 shows feedback construction without URL. ccxt/async_support/base/exchange.py:271-285 shows network-class exception construction WITH URL.
fix: See Finding #1 (Binance) for the remediation. Bybit V5 (Finding #2) is benign as-coded.

title: reset_stalled_portfolio_analytics SECURITY DEFINER lacks REVOKE — cross-tenant data tampering / DoS via any authenticated caller
summary: New SECURITY DEFINER function reset_stalled_portfolio_analytics(INTERVAL) is created with NO REVOKE EXECUTE FROM PUBLIC and NO targeted GRANT. By default Postgres grants EXECUTE to PUBLIC on CREATE FUNCTION, meaning ANY authenticated user (or anon) can invoke it. Because it runs as postgres (SECDEF) it UPDATEs portfolio_analytics across all tenants. The p_stale_threshold is caller-controlled with only a `> 0` check — a caller can pass interval '1 second' to reap virtually all 'computing' rows across every tenant, flipping them to status='failed' with a watchdog error message. This is cross-tenant data tampering plus a DoS against in-flight portfolio analytics computations. The sibling pattern reset_stalled_compute_jobs in mig 20260412094449_compute_jobs_admin_and_defer.sql has the correct REVOKE — this is a clear convention deviation.
evidence: Migration body contains `CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION reset_stalled_portfolio_analytics(p_stale_threshold INTERVAL DEFAULT interval '30 minutes') ... SECURITY DEFINER ... UPDATE portfolio_analytics SET computation_status='failed' ... WHERE computation_status='computing' AND computed_at < (now() - p_stale_threshold);`. grep -c 'GRANT|REVOKE' 20260516122247_portfolio_analytics_stuck_row_reaper.sql returns 0. Reference function reset_stalled_compute_jobs at supabase/migrations/20260412094449_compute_jobs_admin_and_defer.sql contains `REVOKE ALL ON FUNCTION reset_stalled_compute_jobs FROM PUBLIC, anon, authenticated;`.
fix: Append after the COMMENT ON FUNCTION block at L61: `REVOKE ALL ON FUNCTION public.reset_stalled_portfolio_analytics(INTERVAL) FROM PUBLIC, anon, authenticated;` Also consider adding `GRANT EXECUTE ON FUNCTION public.reset_stalled_portfolio_analytics(INTERVAL) TO service_role;` if Railway worker calls via service-role token. Add a self-verifying DO block at file end that calls `PERFORM public._assert_no_public_execute('public.reset_stalled_portfolio_analytics(interval)');` to match the project's standard SECDEF hardening pattern.

title: _assert_strategy_visible_to_allocator orphan-org bypass is more permissive than RLS strategies_org_read — small cross-tenant exposure path
summary: The visibility helper returns TRUE if the strategy's owning org has zero members (orphan org). The comment justifies this as 'avoid frozen scenario commits when the owning org was post-sanitize emptied'. However, this is more permissive than the production RLS predicate strategies_org_read (supabase/migrations/20260409212457_fix_organization_rls_recursion.sql L95-L100) which only admits org-scoped strategies to members of that org via is_org_member — and is_org_member (same file L25-L37) has no orphan-org relaxation. The trigger therefore admits voluntary_add / bridge_recommended INSERTs referencing orphan-org strategies that the calling allocator could NOT SELECT via RLS. Combined with the trigger's RAISE EXCEPTION (L126-L128) echoing the strategy_id back to the caller on failure, this creates a probe path: an allocator can call commit_scenario_batch with an arbitrary strategy_id and learn (a) it exists and is org-scoped, or (b) it exists in an orphan org (INSERT succeeds bypassing browse RLS). Lower severity because the orphan-org condition is rare and post-sanitize narrative is plausible — but the divergence from RLS is a quiet expansion of attack surface that operators should approve explicitly.
evidence: L82-L84: `IF v_org_member_count = 0 THEN RETURN TRUE; END IF;`. Compare to is_org_member at supabase/migrations/20260409212457_fix_organization_rls_recursion.sql L25-L37 which checks `EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM organization_members WHERE organization_id = org_id AND user_id = auth.uid())` with no orphan-org branch.
fix: Option A (recommended): tighten the helper to match RLS — drop the zero-member fast-path and require allocator membership unconditionally. The post-sanitize orphan scenario is rare enough to warrant a manual admin remediation rather than a permanent code path. Option B: keep the relaxation but add an audit_log emission `log_audit_event_service(allocator_id, 'scenario.commit.orphan_org_bypass', 'strategy', p_strategy_id, jsonb_build_object('org_id', v_org_id))` inside the orphan branch so operators can spot exploitation. Option C: also gate on `strategies.status = 'published'` in the same SELECT (currently the helper does NOT check status — L66-L68 only reads organization_id).

title: _assert_strategy_visible_to_allocator does not check strategies.status — defense-in-depth gap
summary: The visibility helper reads only strategies.organization_id (L66-L68). It does NOT check strategies.status. The comment at L64-L65 says 'globally visible while published' but the code does not enforce 'while published'. The upstream commit_scenario_batch RPC does separately check v_strategy_status='published' (mig 082 L153-L159 + bridge_recommended branch), so the gap is currently closed by the RPC. But the trigger is presented as a defense-in-depth gate that fires 'regardless of which RPC or route is inserting' (L13-L15 comment). A future writer that forgets the status check would bypass status enforcement at the trigger. Lower severity because match_decisions INSERT is gated by RLS to admin + service_role, so casual code paths can't bypass.
evidence: L66-L68: `SELECT organization_id INTO v_org_id FROM strategies WHERE id = p_strategy_id;` — no status column read, no status filter. Helper returns TRUE for any organization_id IS NULL strategy regardless of status.
fix: Extend the SELECT to read status: `SELECT organization_id, status INTO v_org_id, v_status FROM strategies WHERE id = p_strategy_id;` and add `IF v_status IS DISTINCT FROM 'published' THEN RETURN FALSE; END IF;` after the not-found short-circuit. The 'archived' / 'draft' / 'pending_review' shapes should never pass visibility for non-owners.

title: _match_decisions_visibility_check trigger error message confirms strategy existence to non-member allocator (small enumeration leak)
summary: On visibility failure the trigger raises 42501 with `strategy % is not visible to allocator % (org-scoped, allocator not a member)`. This echoes back the user-supplied strategy_id but ALSO confirms the strategy exists and is org-scoped (vs nonexistent — which short-circuits at the helper's IF v_org_id IS NULL returning TRUE). An attacker that fuzzes UUIDs through commit_scenario_batch can distinguish 'strategy exists but not visible' (this error) from 'strategy doesn't exist' (different downstream RPC error from IF v_strategy_status IS NULL). This is a tiny enumeration oracle. Existing strategies_org_read RLS already lets users probe existence via SELECT 404 vs 200, so the marginal disclosure is small — but for an explicit hardening migration it's worth tightening.
evidence: L126-L130: `RAISE EXCEPTION 'match_decisions visibility check: strategy % is not visible to allocator % (org-scoped, allocator not a member). audit-2026-05-07 M-0825.', NEW.strategy_id, NEW.allocator_id USING ERRCODE = 'insufficient_privilege';`
fix: Replace the org-specific message with a generic one: `RAISE EXCEPTION 'match_decisions: strategy not visible to allocator' USING ERRCODE = 'insufficient_privilege';` — log the strategy_id + allocator_id + reason via PERFORM public.log_audit_event_service(...) before the RAISE so operators retain forensic evidence without leaking to the client.

title: Non-SECDEF helpers (_assert_retention_columns, _validate_scenario_diff, _match_decisions_visibility_check) lack SET search_path — minor convention drift
summary: The `_assert_retention_columns` helper is LANGUAGE plpgsql but lacks `SET search_path = public, pg_catalog`. Same pattern in `_validate_scenario_diff` (mig 20260516160800 L49-L56) and `_match_decisions_visibility_check` (mig 20260516160700 L114-L117). None of these are SECURITY DEFINER so the bypass risk is low (search_path manipulation only affects SECDEF privilege boundary). However the project convention across 89 prior migrations is to lock search_path explicitly on every migration-installed function. Informational — call out for convention parity. Not exploitable because none of these have SECDEF, no dynamic SQL, and most are REVOKEd from app roles.
evidence: L40-L43 of mig 20260516160200: `CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION public._assert_retention_columns() RETURNS VOID LANGUAGE plpgsql AS $fn$` — no SET search_path. Same shape in mig 20260516160800 L49-L56 (_validate_scenario_diff) and mig 20260516160700 L114-L117 (_match_decisions_visibility_check). Compare to sanitize_user mig 20260516160100 L41-L42 which uses LANGUAGE plpgsql SECURITY DEFINER SET search_path = public, pg_catalog.
fix: Add `SET search_path = public, pg_catalog` between LANGUAGE plpgsql and AS $fn$/$trg$ in all three function definitions. Cheap convention parity that aids future audits.

title: playwright-report artifact (7-day retention) leaks real test-Supabase NEXT_PUBLIC_* creds when seed-gated e2e specs fail
summary: The pre-merge gate verified that the nextjs-build artifact only contains placeholders (Path 1) and that the seed-gated rebuilt .next/ never gets uploaded (Path 2). It did NOT examine the playwright-report artifact uploaded on any e2e failure. When the seed-gated suite (onboarding-funnel / discovery-* / strategy-v2-*) fails, Playwright's on-failure screenshot capture + on-first-retry trace capture (playwright.config.ts:11-12) record the running browser's state: HAR-format network requests with the Bearer anon-key header in Authorization, full request URLs containing the test project ref (qmnijlgmdhviwzwfyzlc.supabase.co), and screenshots showing the page source — all of which embed the real TEST_SUPABASE_URL and TEST_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY that the rebuild step at L640-L669 inlined into client bundles. The artifact has 7-day retention vs nextjs-build's 1-day, widening the exfil window for any third-party action subsequently added to this workflow with actions:read. The PR's belt+suspenders model (workflow permissions:contents:read implies actions:none, AND clean artifact) is preserved for nextjs-build but only the belt (permissions:none on actions) protects playwright-report — there is no scrubbing of the trace content. This is the same artifact-leak class the PR claims to close, via a different artifact name.
evidence: .github/workflows/ci.yml:725-731 (Upload Playwright report on failure, retention-days: 7) is the only upload that fires after the seed-gated rebuild at L640-L669. playwright.config.ts:11-12 sets trace:'on-first-retry' and screenshot:'only-on-failure', both of which capture the rebuilt browser bundle's NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL/ANON_KEY in network traces and rendered HTML. The seed-gated step at L716-L717 also exports the same secrets into the runner env, so anything tracing process.env (e.g., a debug console.log) would surface them too.
fix: Either (a) drop the artifact upload when the seed-gated path is involved (gate it on `if: failure() && vars.E2E_TEST_DB_CONFIGURED != 'true'`, accepting that real-DB failures lose the trace for debugging — recovery: re-run after wiring a non-secret debug surface), or (b) post-process the playwright-report dir before upload: `find playwright-report -name '*.zip' -o -name '*.html' -o -name '*.txt' -o -name '*.json' | xargs sed -i "s|${TEST_SUPABASE_URL}|<redacted-url>|g; s|${TEST_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY}|<redacted-key>|g"` (note: trace.zip is binary so the substitution needs unzip → sed → rezip), or (c) write to a separate artifact name when the seed-gated path ran and route those to a private-readers-only S3 bucket. Option (a) is simplest and matches the PR's own '(c) preferred: keep the credentials off the artifact path entirely' philosophy.

title: nightly-pdf-report artifact (14-day retention) leaks DEMO_PDF_SECRET if the staging probe fails with the secret in a network trace
summary: Symmetrical issue to playwright-report in ci.yml. The portfolio-pdf-demo Playwright spec hits the staging /demo PDF endpoint with DEMO_PDF_SECRET in the auth path (query param, header, or cookie — the spec doesn't appear in the diff but the secret is exported into env at L40). On failure, the Playwright report uploaded as nightly-pdf-report (retention-days:14) will contain the secret in the trace's network log of the failing request. The retention is DOUBLE the ci.yml playwright-report (14d vs 7d), widening the window. DEMO_PDF_SECRET is a deliberate auth credential for the production-staging demo PDF endpoint — leak = unauthorized demo PDF generation against staging.
evidence: .github/workflows/nightly.yml:40 (DEMO_PDF_SECRET in env), L56 (npx playwright test invocation), L57-L63 (Upload playwright report on failure, retention-days:14). Coupled with playwright.config.ts:11-12 default trace + screenshot capture.
fix: Same pattern as the ci.yml finding: scrub the secret out of the report before upload, or drop the report upload entirely (a nightly probe failure with no trace is recoverable via re-run with PWDEBUG=1 locally). Minimum: shorten retention-days from 14 to 1 — the issue auto-filed at L64-L90 already encodes the run URL, so a maintainer can re-run within 24h and capture a fresh trace locally with the secret already in their environment.

title: Rebuild step preserves .next/cache (placeholder-populated webpack cache) — risk of stale-cred residue if Next.js's cache invalidation doesn't roll on DefinePlugin env change
summary: The rebuild-with-real-env step wipes .next/server, .next/static, and 3 manifest JSON files, but DELIBERATELY preserves .next/cache (the incremental webpack/Turbopack store, per the inline comment at L658-L660). Next.js relies on webpack's DefinePlugin to inline process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_* at compile time. If webpack's per-module cache key does NOT include the resolved DefinePlugin literals (and there are known cases where DefinePlugin-injected literals are not part of the module hash), the second build would re-use placeholder-compiled chunks from .next/cache for modules that read NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL — producing a hybrid .next/ where SOME chunks have placeholders and OTHERS have real creds. The hybrid is still functionally correct enough to pass the seed-gated specs (the real URL has to land in at least the Supabase-client module, and that module's source DOES change because env-derived literals appear in the emitted text), but it makes the security model fragile: a webpack/Next.js cache-key change could either (a) silently route real creds into the chunks that DO get cached + uploaded as nextjs-build via a future regression, or (b) silently route placeholders into the chunks the seed-gated specs depend on, causing flaky test-skip cascades. The mitigation comment at L659 says 'rebuild is incremental on top of the placeholder run' — that IS the concern.
evidence: ci.yml:656-662 — `rm -rf .next/server .next/static .next/build-manifest.json .next/prerender-manifest.json .next/required-server-files.json 2>/dev/null || true` deliberately leaves .next/cache, .next/BUILD_ID, .next/routes-manifest.json, .next/app-build-manifest.json (if present), and .next/diagnostics intact. None of these are uploaded under the current artifact glob (.next/** !.next/cache/**), but a future glob change or a future routes-manifest-as-artifact would inherit the risk.
fix: Wipe the entire .next/ before the rebuild: `rm -rf .next/server .next/static .next/cache .next/build-manifest.json .next/prerender-manifest.json .next/required-server-files.json .next/routes-manifest.json .next/app-build-manifest.json .next/app-path-routes-manifest.json .next/BUILD_ID 2>/dev/null || true`. Cost: the rebuild loses webpack's incremental advantage (~30-60s additional), but the security model becomes monotonic — no chance of hybrid placeholder/real-cred chunks. Document the cost trade-off in the inline comment. Alternatively, document that .next/cache is intentionally placeholder-only-derived and never uploaded — add a CI assertion step that fails the run if .next/cache contains any string matching TEST_SUPABASE_URL.

title: sql-tests preflight meta-command regex misses \connect, \password, \timing, \setenv — psql client surface broader than the 4 patterns enumerated
summary: The Finding-6 preflight (audit-2026-05-07 red-team) refuses \!, \copy, \COPY, and \o. The psql client has additional meta-commands with security-relevant behavior: \connect (switches DB, can target prod if DSN includes prod-shaped credentials in $PGPASSWORD), \password (interactive password-set, would hang the job and could re-export to a connected superuser session), \timing (low-risk, but combined with \copy in an ALSO-trapped pattern is moot), \setenv (modify the psql process env from inside the SQL file — could leak via subsequent \copy or \!), \out (alias for \o on older psql versions), \g (with file argument writes query output to file — \g file is functionally equivalent to \o + execute), \i (include external file — could read /etc/passwd into the query buffer + emit via NOTICE). Each is a CLIENT-side meta-command unreachable from server-side check_function defenses. The current preflight only catches 4 of ~10 known-dangerous patterns.
evidence: ci.yml:308-316 lists the rejected meta-commands. PostgreSQL psql documentation lists \connect, \setenv, \g <file>, \i, \password as additional file-IO / env-mutating client commands. The preflight uses plain grep -E so adding patterns is a one-line change per pattern.
fix: Extend the preflight regex set: also reject \connect, \setenv, \g <file> (\\g[[:space:]]+[^[:space:]]+), \i (\\i\\b), \password, \out. Add inline comment explaining each pattern's threat. Note: legitimate test files would use plain SELECT/RAISE EXCEPTION constructs; none of the existing supabase/tests/test_*.sql files use any of these patterns (verifiable via `grep -l '\\\\\(connect\|setenv\|i\\b\|g [^[:space:]]\|password\|out\)' supabase/tests/test_*.sql`).

title: actions/cache restore opens a cache-poisoning channel — Playwright bundle cache is keyed only on @playwright/test package.json, not on the cache's own content
summary: The Cache Playwright browsers step uses key `${{ runner.os }}-playwright-${{ hashFiles('node_modules/@playwright/test/package.json') }}` with a permissive restore-keys ladder `${{ runner.os }}-playwright-`. GitHub's actions/cache writes ONLY from the default branch (push events to main) by default — PR runs read but cannot write. So an attacker would need to land a malicious PR to poison the cache. But the workflow runs npx playwright install with-deps chromium only on cache-miss (L535-L541), so a poisoned cache would NEVER be re-validated against upstream. Combined with the restore-keys fallback, this lets a malicious push to main commit an empty/altered ~/.cache/ms-playwright that subsequent runs would silently inherit. Defense: pin a content-hash-derived key (e.g., include a SHA of an expected chromium-build manifest), or run with --force-reinstall on first build of a new day. Severity LOW because (a) main-branch PRs go through code review, (b) the poisoned-cache attacker would already have main-branch write access, (c) the impact is limited to test-environment integrity not prod-cred exfil.
evidence: ci.yml:519-524 — cache key uses only @playwright/test package.json hash + permissive restore-keys ladder. ci.yml:535-541 — `if cache-hit == true: skip install`. There is no fingerprint verification of the restored ~/.cache/ms-playwright contents.
fix: Either (a) drop the restore-keys ladder so a cache hit requires exact-key match, or (b) verify the restored binary's SHA against a checked-in expected value before trusting the cache, or (c) run `npx playwright install chromium --dry-run` and bail if it reports a mismatch. Option (a) is one-line — delete L523-L524.

title: No Dependabot config for github-actions ecosystem — SHA pins will become stale and miss upstream security fixes
summary: All 10 actions are now SHA-pinned (good). But pinning is anti-monotonic: every bump now requires a manual `gh api repos/<org>/<action>/git/ref/tags/<tag>` lookup + commit. Without Dependabot or Renovate watching the github-actions ecosystem, the SHAs will drift from upstream. Real-world impact: when actions/checkout v4.3.2 ships with a fix for, e.g., a credential-helper escape, this repo would remain on v4.3.1 indefinitely. The PR description acknowledges this (deferred-finding list mentions 'Dependabot for github-actions ecosystem') but the deferral has no tracking issue or follow-up commit. LOW severity because the current SHAs are clean as of merge date, but the half-life of GitHub Actions security fixes is days, so this turns into MEDIUM within ~3-6 months.
evidence: No .github/dependabot.yml in the merged tree (verified via find .github -type f). The deferred-finding list at docs/runbooks/ci-hardening-review-cluster-gate-2026-05-16.md mentions 'Dependabot for github-actions ecosystem' but with no GitHub issue link, no PR-N0 owner.
fix: Add .github/dependabot.yml with `package-ecosystem: github-actions, schedule.interval: weekly`. Dependabot understands SHA pins and emits PRs that bump both the SHA and the trailing semver comment. One-time setup, two-line config.

title: No automated post-merge gate that asserts the artifact does not contain TEST_SUPABASE_URL — verification is a manual checklist in the runbook
summary: The runbook documents a manual post-merge spot check: `gh run download <run-id> -n nextjs-build -D /tmp/check && grep -r 'TEST_SUPABASE_URL\|test-supabase\|qmnijlgmdhviwzwfyzlc' /tmp/check/.next/ | wc -l` should return 0. There is no CI step that enforces this. If a future PR re-introduces the seed-aware ternary at L184-L186 (or moves the rebuild step BEFORE the upload), CI would still pass green — the regression would only surface if a maintainer remembered to run the manual check. The PR's deferred-finding list explicitly mentions 'automated artifact-content gate' as a follow-up but with no owner or timeline.
evidence: docs/runbooks/ci-hardening-permissions-c0293.md:107-114 — manual verification only. No automated grep in any workflow step.
fix: Add a post-upload assertion step in the frontend-build job: after the upload-artifact step, re-grep the just-uploaded directory tree for the placeholder + the test-DB project ref. If any non-placeholder match surfaces, fail the build. Concrete shape: `grep -rE '(TEST_SUPABASE_URL|qmnijlgmdhviwzwfyzlc|TEST_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY)' .next/ public/ && exit 1 || true`. Cost: ~5 seconds per build, monotonic security improvement.
